data.title: Trump's very, very, very simple way of calculating reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
By Steve Goldstein

White House calculation: Every 4% increase in tariffs to leads to 1% rise in import good prices

President Donald Trump's announcement of reciprocal tariffs - with that massive chart behind him - drew puzzled reactions initially.

How did Vietnam - ostensibly a country that the U.S. would want to cultivate to isolate leading adversary China - get hit with one of the highest tariff rates? How come key ally Japan got slapped with a 24% tariff? How did Israel, which just lowered its tariffs on U.S. goods to literally zero, get hit with a 17% tariff?

Why is a country inhabited by penguins tariffed at all?

Sure, there are things like non-tariff barriers - regulations, sales taxes, currency manipulation - but the White House formula for reciprocal tariffs was much simpler.

It's just what is that country's goods exports to the U.S. divided by the country's trade balance. Then, divide that by two. And don't set any number below 10%.

"Put simply, the bigger the nominal trade deficit a country has with the U.S. adjusted for the absolute size of that country's imports, the bigger the tariff," says George Saravelos, head of currency research at Deutsche Bank.

The U.S. Trade Representative has its own description of the calculation but it amounts to exactly the same thing.

Technically, it makes two additional adjustments.

First, it assumes the price elasticity of import demand is 4 - so, a 1% increase in price would lead to a 4% drop in demand.

Then, and this is an important one, it assumes that every 1% increase in tariffs leads to a 0.25% increase in import prices.

Put a different way, using Morgan Stanley's calculation that the effective tariff rate is now 22%, a rise of roughly 20 points from 2024 levels, that should lead to a 5% surge in import prices - according to Trump administration numbers.

Going back to the USTR reciprocal tariff calculation - 4 multiplied by 0.25% is 1, so, the factors offset each other.

The most ironic part of the USTR message comes in the citations. It cites a paper with this title: "Globalization and the gains from variety" - not a message that was articulated on Wednesday.

U.S. stock market futures were pointing to an ugly start to trade on Thursday, with futures on the Dow industrials (YM00) losing 1,000 points.

-Steve Goldstein

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-03-25 0508ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Rolls-Royce in Talks for U.K. Subsidies for New Plane Engine, FT Says, Citing Sources
data.description:
--Rolls-Royce is in talks with the British government about getting taxpayer support to help fund the development of a new plane engine for the next generation of narrow-body aircraft primarily used for short-haul flights, the Financial Times reports, citing unnamed sources.

--It isn't clear how much of the estimated 3 billion pounds ($3.90 billion) in development cost the U.K. engineering and defense company is seeking in assistance from the government, but Rolls-Royce is ready to invest significant sums itself into the project, FT reports.

--Rolls-Royce declined to comment to the FT on the government talks. 




  Full story: htTps://shorturl.at/5Ybhw 





  Write to Pierre Bertrand at pierre.bertrand@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 03, 2025 05:10 ET (09:10 GMT)

data.title: 'I'm not trying to be overly doomsdayish': My grandmother is in her 70s and has $400K in stocks. Should she sell?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'She has a $250,000 house, on which she has a mortgage with a fairly low interest rate'

April is National Financial Literacy Month. To mark the occasion, MarketWatch will publish a series of "Financial Fitness" articles to help readers improve their fiscal health, and offer advice on how to save, invest and spend their money wisely. Read more here.

Dear Quentin,

My grandmother is in her late 70s and has about $400,000 investments in the stock market. She has a $250,000 house, on which she has a mortgage with a fairly low interest rate. She has retirement savings and a pension and has never had to pull from her investments.

I'm not trying to harp too much on the current economic state, but I'm a little worried about her losing her investments and still owing money on her home. I'm sorry in advance if this sounds silly or political, and 100% I'm not trying to be overly doomsdayish.

I just want to be sure she won't end up in a situation with nothing, and I am trying to look out for her. She skimps on a lot of things and doesn't have a huge amount of savings in her bank - I'd say she has $5,000. She's had her ups and downs, but overall she is in good health.

At this point, would it make more sense for her to have her home paid off?

The Grandkid

Related: I invested $100,000 in the S&P 500 in February and lost $10,000. How long will it take to recover?

Dear Grandkid,

No wolf, imaginary or otherwise, is scratching at your grandmother's door. Yes, it would make sense.

I received the exact opposite question from a reader recently, wondering whether it was too late for his mother, who is 63, to invest in the stock market. As I told them, a diversified portfolio includes cash, stocks, bonds and non-U.S. stocks. Currently, it looks like 61% of your grandmother's wealth is in stocks, 38% is in real estate and less than 1% is in cash. Ideally, it would be nice if she had more money in an emergency fund. Mortgage payments are not seen as bad debt, but it could also be good to lift that weight from her shoulders. I'd prefer to see 30% in stocks and 30% in cash.

That said, she has amassed a $400,000 portfolio over the last 50 years because she has played the long game. Don't buckle on the last lap. The good news is that your grandmother's investments, as they currently stand, appear to far outweigh the remainder on her mortgage - as long as she did not take out a reverse mortgage in recent years. I'm assuming that the $400,000 in stocks and $5,000 in a savings account accounts for 100% of her net worth (that, and her $250,000 home). That along with her retirement income puts her in pretty good shape.

It appears that your grandmother can continue to make her mortgage payments and live comfortably.

You can occupy a middle ground while you help your grandmother plan her investments and organize her estate for the next 10 or 20 years. I assume your concern is related to the geopolitical situation - the wars in Ukraine and Gaza, and U.S. foreign policy that appears to be breaking with the postwar Western alliance - and to the stock-market volatility in recent months, which is the result at least in part of the Trump administration's proposed tariffs (which may be part of a negotiating strategy; expectations around these appear to be changing weekly or even daily).

You don't say how much your grandmother is paying every month toward her mortgage, whether she has an interest rate below, say, 5%, or how much of her monthly Social Security and pension checks go toward her mortgage. But if she does become sick later in life - and she's still young, if she's in good health by today's standards - and she does not have long-term-care insurance, she would have to spend down all her assets before applying for Medicaid. Medicaid does not have a five-year look-back or "clawback" rule for paying off a mortgage.

Related: 'Is it finally time to freak out?' I'm in my 50s and worried about the $650K in my 401(k).

Martin Schamis, a certified financial planner with Janney Montgomery Scott in Philadelphia, commends your grandmother's prudence and sensible long-term investing, but he has another tax reminder: "Investment assets held in nonqualified accounts will receive a step-up in basis at the death of the owner. While you mention your grandmother is thankfully in good health, this should be considered along with the actual makeup of the investments, the aforementioned embedded gain and your grandmother's need to access the funds."

Given that your grandmother has not needed to pull from her investments thanks to having a healthy pension and retirement funds, Schamis adds, it also makes it more likely that she can continue to make her mortgage payments and live comfortably. "Not having to withdraw from the portfolio in the short term to cover everyday expenses means that she should be less sensitive to the intermittent moves in her investments," he says. "You don't mention the cost basis of her investments, but it sounds as if she's been a long-term investor."

Your grandmother has amassed a $400,000 portfolio over the last 50 years. Don't buckle on the last lap.

Your grandmother could live well into her 90s, so don't make any big, drastic decisions that you may regret later or that could result in a massive capital-gains tax bill. For every investor who is worried about the recent stock-market tumble, there will be another bullish investor who is cheering on changes President Donald Trump is making to U.S. foreign and domestic policy and looking at an uptick in the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA, S&P 500 SPX and Nasdaq COMP and saying, "I told you so!" That investor may have great confidence in the years ahead.

"As many taxpayers sit on long-term positions with unrealized gains - think Nvidia (NVDA) - selling can trigger multiple levels of federal and state taxes, depending on one's situation," says Michael Brennan, a tax adviser with Berkowitz Pollack Brant Advisors and CPAs in New York City. "Additionally, the opportunity cost of a poorly timed and misjudged portfolio drawdown could severely impact long-term financial performance. The age-old saying 'You can't time the market' remains relevant more than ever in today's market conditions."

Related: Will Trump's 'liberation day' tariffs hurt my retirement? I plan to retire in 3 years.

Certain advisable strategies can help mitigate the tax effect of drawing down on positions, Brennan says. "Selling positions within an IRA are generally tax free," he notes. "Starting with positions that have little to no gain or loss is advantageous for taxpayers who want to move more into cash. Harvesting losses from other sources against gains should be analyzed and timed properly to ensure tax efficiency. For taxpayers who are charitably inclined, donating highly appreciated long-term positions can provide a tax deduction without incurring a taxable gain."

Some encouraging data points for bears to chew over: ??The U.S. currently has near full employment. Unemployment was 4.1% in February, up just a tick from 4% in January. Gross domestic product growth in the second quarter outperformed many economists' more cautious predictions. The labor-participation rate hovers around 80%. The annual inflation rate hit 2.8% in February, down from a recent peak of 9.1% in June 2022. Wage growth is still solid, even if consumer spending is showing signs of slowing down.

There will always be an investor who predicts good times, and another who predicts a doomsday scenario.

And some cautious bites for bulls: The federal deficit is now more than 6% of GDP, double what it was a decade ago. The Federal Reserve appears reluctant to make any significant interest-rate cuts anytime soon. The gap between the rich and everyone else has widened, along with their spending habits: The 10% of U.S. households that earn $250,000 or more annually now account for nearly 50% of consumer spending. Consumer confidence in March hit a four-year low. It suggests a worrying dual economy where the other 90% are struggling to make ends meet.

There will always be an investor who predicts good times, and another who predicts doomsday. Economist Harry Dent told Fox News last year: "It is going to be the biggest crash of our lifetime, most of it happening in 2024." Last month, he again predicted a "2008-level market crash." Ditto those who predict great things ahead. Oppenheimer, known as the biggest bull on Wall Street, started the year by predicting the S&P would rise 17%. If they keep predicting good or bad things ahead, who knows - they both may eventually be right.

You and your grandmother don't need to be either bulls or bears. You just need to safeguard her financial stability.

Related: 'I'm absolutely terrible at managing money': I'm 35 with zero savings and $8,000 debt. How do I turn my life around?

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'I'm deeply disturbed': My portfolio lost 20%. With Trump's trade war, do I sell my stocks and buy gold?

My wife and I found two stocks with a 20% return. We have $75,000. Should we invest it in the S&P 500 or individual stocks?

'I'm deeply disturbed': My portfolio lost 20%. With Trump's trade war, do I sell my stocks and buy gold?

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

04-03-25 0500ET

Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: How much do YOU pay for rent, Caleb Simpson? TikTok star who tours homes talks about his own place.
data.description:
By Kelsi Karruli

Simpson is moving to a new place-but he says he's relishing the time he has left in the apartment that has seen him through some phenomenal times.

Caleb Simpson has made a career out of nosying around the apartments of celebrities, business leaders, and strangers he runs into on the street-in both his hometown of New York City and farther-flung destinations like Los Angeles and Alabama.

But now, the 33-year-old is turning the tables, this time welcoming Realtor.com into his own abode for the latest installment of Celebrity Sanctuary, while revealing the answer to his iconic catchphrase question: "How much do you pay for rent in New York?"

The answer: $2,100 a month for his share of a $6,500 two-story apartment in Brooklyn, which he moved into with some friends during COVID-19, when he thought he would spend just one year there.

Four years-and some 500 TikTok videos-later, he's finally ready to move on, but not before showcasing the home where he has built his career as a real estate influencer in a final swan song.

It marks a milestone moment for Simpson, who has been granted access to the homes of some of the world's biggest stars in recent years, including Scarlett Johansson, Jared Leto, and Christie Brinkley, amassing more than 8.3 million followers in the process.

The North Carolina native's easy confidence on camera carries behind the scenes-and he admits that peace and quiet have never really been his thing. Instead, his Brooklyn dwelling has become a hot spot for parties and even boasts a dedicated "entertaining hot spot," complete with lasers, party lights, and more.

But when it comes to finding some space from the hustle and bustle of New York, there's only one place where Simpson heads: his rooftop deck, which serves as something of a private sanctuary.

In six weeks, Simpson will move to a new abode-but he says he's relishing the time he has left in the apartment that has seen him through some phenomenal times, as well as his fair share of heartbreak.

For now, he's treasuring the memories he's made alongside his roommates (one of whom recently welcomed a baby, adding another member to their group), before moving on to pastures new.

One thing's for sure, however: The entertaining hot spot will follow him wherever he goes.

Caleb Simpson's sanctuary

Caleb Simpson: I moved into this space four years ago with friends. We got it because it was during COVID, and we figured a bunch of friends should move into an apartment together because we couldn't hang out with anybody.

Four years later and I am still here. I have just enjoyed it. There are two outdoor spaces, I love the neighborhood, I just like living here.

I played a very small role in designing it. It's looked a couple of different directions based on who's lived here, because everybody comes in and kind of has their own style and their own furniture that they're bringing from different apartments. Right now, down in this living room, I don't own any of this furniture.

I just rock with it. One of my favorite corners is the cuddle cushion, which has a fireplace and is where I cuddled with Drew Barrymore.

More from Realtor.com: A Glam Home in a Hamptons Trailer Park That Has Become a Summer Playground for the Elite Has Listed for $1.2M

My bedroom was my first attempt at stylizing a room, and it's not great. Homes and spaces hold a lot of emotional weight, and I've lived in this room with my ex-girlfriend and then we broke up, and she took all her stuff and left. So this room has a lot of emotional weight for me, so I'm excited to be leaving it. I changed it completely.

I got inspired going to Japan, and then I got tatami mats. I slept on the ground for a while, and then I got into Japanese art.

You have to really change the space to change the emotional weight, but I do love it because it's right next to this deck, which is my favorite part because I can just wake up and go right outside and use my cold plunge-when it's working-and my sauna.

More from Realtor.com: L.A. Has Approved 4 Permits To Rebuild in Pacific Palisades After Wildfires-as New Video Shows Process of Preparing Properties for Rebuilding

The deck just doesn't really feel like you're in New York; it's a little escape from the city.

I always have people over. Before my roommate had a baby, we were always hosting dinner parties, like once or twice a week.

I even have an entertaining hot spot, which has a bunch of lasers and everything you need to turn your space into a club.

When the clubs weren't open, we made everyone take COVID tests, and they had to pass and then they were allowed into the party. And we kept it to only 20 people that we knew, and then we bought everything we needed to turn it into our own club.

More from Realtor.com: Meghan Markle Makes Surprise Visit to Gwyneth Paltrow's $5 Million Montecito Home as They Try To Stamp Out Rift Rumors

The cops were called a couple of times, but we haven't really thrown down like that in a while.

When I move, I am actually going to miss the bathroom the most because it's beautiful. The floors used to be heated, but they aren't anymore, and Drew actually was the one who told me they were called penny tiles.

More from Realtor.com: Housing Prices Are Pushing Down Payments to New Highs-See How Much More Homebuyers Are Paying

I'll be in this apartment for about six more weeks, and I want my new space to be in Brooklyn-my dream neighborhood is Fort Greene-and I want to be in a pre-war building because modern buildings aren't homey.

This story originally ran on Realtor.com.

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-03-25 0501ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: ARAL CULTURE SUMMIT 2025: UZBEKISTAN'S LANDMARK EVENT ALIGNING CULTURE, ECOLOGY, AND REGIONAL COOPERATION ANNOUNCES OFFICIAL PROGRAMME
data.description:
ARAL CULTURE SUMMIT 2025: UZBEKISTAN'S LANDMARK EVENT ALIGNING CULTURE, ECOLOGY, AND REGIONAL COOPERATION ANNOUNCES OFFICIAL PROGRAMME

ARAL CULTURE SUMMIT 2025: UZBEKISTAN'S LANDMARK EVENT ALIGNING CULTURE, ECOLOGY, AND REGIONAL COOPERATION ANNOUNCES OFFICIAL PROGRAMME

PR Newswire

NUKUS, Uzbekistan, April 3, 2025

LINK to images

  • An emergent initiative dedicated to the social and environmental transformation of the Aral Sea region through art, culture, design and science debuts 4-6 April 2025.
  • To be inaugurated during the first Global Climate Forum in Samarkand on 4th April, attended by EU and Central Asian leaders and reinforcing Uzbekistan's commitment to green development and global cooperation.
  • A multidisciplinary programme in Nukus, Karakalpakstan (5-6 April) with global and regional experts on culture, architecture, ecology, food security and water resource management to foster dialogue and call to action.
  • A masterplan to revitalise Istiqlol, the Summit's future headquarters - a former amusement park and one of few remaining green spaces in Nukus - will be unveiled on 5th April 2025.

NUKUS, Uzbekistan, April 3, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Aral Culture Summit (ACS), a cultural and environmental initiative to revitalise the Aral Sea region in Central Asia will take place from 4-6 April 2025. Organised by the Uzbekistan Art and Culture Development Foundation (ACDF), the Summit seeks to foster dialogue and action through art, culture, design, and science.

ACS will bring together leading cultural figures, policymakers, artists, and environmental experts across two cities to explore and implement sustainable solutions that address the Aral Sea crisis and global climate challenges. Aral Culture Summit 2025 will be inaugurated during the first Global Climate Forum in Samarkand (4th April), attended by regional and European heads-of-states and reinforcing Uzbekistan's commitment to the regional cooperation for environmental and social development.

Programme Highlights and Themes
Aral Culture Summit continues in Nukus, Karakalpakstan near the Aral Sea on 5-6 April and will host a multidisciplinary programme with panel discussions, keynotes, artistic performances, and exhibitions, addressing critical issues such as environmental regeneration, creative economy, and cultural diplomacy. On 5th April, Gayane Umerova, Chairperson of ACDF will announce a preliminary masterplan for the regeneration of Istiqlol, a former amusement park in Nukus, reimagined by Ludi Architects as a permanent cultural and ecological hub.

Gayane Umerova, Chairperson of ACDF, comments:
"Aral Culture Summit is a platform for change. By integrating cultural dialogue with the climate agenda, we are ensuring that the arts contribute to our collective commitment to long term sustainable development. Our participation in the first Global Climate Forum underscores the critical role of culture to drive environmental transformation."

aralculturesummit.uz | @aral.culture.summit

Press Kit: LINK (including hi-res images)

Photo - https://mma.prnewswire.com/media/2656559/Aral_Culture_Summit_1.jpg
Photo - https://mma.prnewswire.com/media/2656556/Aral_Culture_Summit_2.jpg
Photo - https://mma.prnewswire.com/media/2656555/Aral_Culture_Summit_3.jpg

 

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/aral-culture-summit-2025-uzbekistans-landmark-event-aligning-culture-ecology-and-regional-cooperation-announces-official-programme-302419642.html

SOURCE Aral Culture Summit

data.title: Aryza Announces Strategic Acquisition of RiskLogix Solutions, Expanding Capabilities and Geographic Reach
data.description:
DUBLIN, IRELAND / ACCESS Newswire / April 3, 2025 / Aryza, a global provider of mission-critical automation software across the credit lifecycle, is pleased to announce the acquisition of RiskLogix Solutions, a prominent provider of governance, risk, and compliance (GRC) solutions. This strategic acquisition reinforces Aryza's commitment to delivering best-in-class Credit & Debt Lifecycle Management SaaS solutions and positions both companies for accelerated growth and innovation.

The integration of RiskLogix Solutions into Aryza's portfolio enables significant synergies through an overlapping customer base. Existing customers of both companies will benefit from an expanded suite of products and services, creating a comprehensive solution offering that addresses evolving market demands. By combining the strengths of both businesses, customers can expect enhanced functionality and greater value.

The acquisition will enable RiskLogix Solutions to leverage Aryza's global presence to introduce its innovative solutions to new regions, and lays the foundation for Aryza's future governance, risk, and compliance (GRC) offerings. Aryza is poised to deliver a next-generation GRC platform that meets the demands of an increasingly complex regulatory and business environment.

Colin Brown, CEO of Aryza:

"We are thrilled to welcome RiskLogix Solutions to the Aryza family. This acquisition not only strengthens our product portfolio but also expands our global reach and deepens our technical capabilities. We are accustomed to working in highly regulated sectors, and this acquisition is a natural progression for our business. We have a track record of springboarding smaller, high-potential businesses into the international market, and I look forward to incorporating RiskLogix-RiskLogix Solutions' GRC capabilities into the Aryza product offering."

John Kiddy, CEO of RiskLogix Solutions:

"Joining forces with Aryza marks an exciting new chapter for RiskLogix Solutions. Our shared vision and complementary strengths will enable us to reach new heights and deliver cutting-edge solutions to a broader audience. We look forward to leveraging Aryza's global network to accelerate our growth and bring our offerings to new markets. Aryza and RiskLogix Solutions are committed to ensuring a simple transition for customers and stakeholders moving forward."

About Aryza

Aryza is a global provider of Credit & Debt Lifecycle Management SaaS solutions, specializing in innovative tools that streamline complex processes for Financial Services & Individuals. With a focus on delivering value through technology, Aryza empowers organizations to improve efficiency, compliance, and customer outcomes.

About RiskLogix Solutions

RiskLogix Solutions is a leading provider of Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) solutions, helping businesses enhance decision-making, improve operational resilience, and drive strategic growth. Our innovative tools empower organizations to identify and manage risks proactively, optimize performance, and unlock new business opportunities. With a proven track record, RiskLogix Solutions enables companies to build stronger governance structures, optimise operational efficiency, and achieve corporate objectives.

For media inquiries, please contact:

Ellen Oliver
Ellen.oliver@aryza.com
+44 (0)7842 021620

SOURCE: Aryza



View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Swiss Inflation Holds Steady as Tariffs Set to Buffet Economy — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Ed Frankl 




  Swiss inflation remained unchanged in March, though the impact of U.S. tariffs threaten to roil the country's economy and push the Swiss National Bank to cut rates further. 



  Annual inflation stayed at 0.3%, the same as February, matching the lowest level in almost four years, Switzerland's federal statistics office said Thursday. 



  Trends from increased package-holiday and clothing prices contrasted with declining energy and accommodation prices, it said. Core inflation, which excludes more volatile fresh food and energy prices, remained markedly higher, staying at 0.9% for the third-straight month. 



  But new U.S tariffs announced Wednesday by President Trump are expected to significantly hit the Swiss economy, and potentially bring inflation lower as economic activity slows. The SNB's inflation target is 0%-2%. 



  The Trump administration imposed a 31% tariff on Swiss exports to the U.S., higher than the 20% placed on the neighboring European Union. The U.S. is Switzerland's largest goods-export market. 



  Key Swiss exports, including luxury watches, could be strongly impacted by the tariffs. However, the White House left off another major industry for the Swiss economy--pharmaceuticals--from new duties. Swatch shares fell in Thursday morning trade. 



  The SNB cut its key interest rate in March to 0.25%, citing increased geopolitical uncertainty, as well as risks that inflation would go lower. SNB President Martin Schlegel in recent months hasn't ruled out negative interest rates. 



  The Swiss franc hit a five-month high against the dollar after the tariff announcement, although it has weakened in recent months. But the more important feature for inflation is the negative impact of tariffs on the economy, Capital Economics economist Adrian Prettejohn said in a note. 



  "This means inflation will be a little lower than otherwise over the medium term," he said. He now anticipates the SNB to lower its policy to zero in June, compared with holding at 0.25% as previously expected. The SNB forecasts inflation to average at 0.4% this year. 



  Meanwhile, Swiss President Karin Keller-Sutter said her government took note of the new duties and would decide soon how to proceed. 



  "The country's long-term economic interests are paramount. Respect to international law and free trade remain core values," she said on X. 



  Swiss industry group Economiesuisse said Thursday that the new tariffs were harmful and unjustified, would make Swiss exports more expensive and would weaken competitiveness. 





  Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 03, 2025 04:35 ET (08:35 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Exclusive Interview with CLOU Energy Storage's Overseas GM Songyu He: Key Strategies for Global Expansion
data.description:
Exclusive Interview with CLOU Energy Storage's Overseas GM Songyu He: Key Strategies for Global Expansion

Exclusive Interview with CLOU Energy Storage's Overseas GM Songyu He: Key Strategies for Global Expansion

PR Newswire

SHENZHEN, China, April 3, 2025

SHENZHEN, China, April 3, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Recently, Sohu Finance interviewed Songyu He, General Manager of CLOU Energy Storage Overseas, to discuss the company's global expansion and competitive strategies. Here are the key insights from the conversation.

How is CLOU expanding its overseas markets?

Songyu He: CLOU is focusing on three main regions: strengthening its presence in the Americas, expanding rapidly in Europe, and exploring opportunities in Belt and Road countries. In the Americas, CLOU has built strong local teams across sales, technical support, and after-sales service. In Europe, Germany, the UK, and Italy are key markets, with Spain emerging as a potential new focus.

What challenges does CLOU face in new overseas markets?

Songyu He: Securing the first large-scale project with a reputable local client is often the biggest hurdle. Beyond that, managing the full lifecycle of an energy storage project—from production and logistics to installation, commissioning, and ensuring 20 years of reliable operation—is crucial. CLOU prioritizes long-term brand building and sustainable market development.

How does CLOU navigate intense price competition?

Songyu He: CLOU adopts two key strategies:

1. Advancing Product Innovation with String Energy Storage Systems

The market is shifting from centralized to string energy storage systems. CLOU is developing AC storage cabinets that integrate string inverters and batteries into a single unit, improving efficiency and flexibility. Only a few companies globally can deliver mature string solutions, and CLOU is among them, with a focus on the Americas, Europe, and Japan.

2. Strengthening After-Sales Service

A robust after-sales network is crucial for long-term success. In the U.S., CLOU collaborates with over ten third-party engineering firms to provide maintenance and support across multiple states. In Chile, CLOU has established local service partnerships to support ongoing projects.

How does CLOU view the trend of overseas manufacturing?

Songyu He: While offshore manufacturing presents challenges such as labor costs and supply chain complexity, it can ultimately lower costs, especially when factoring in tariffs. CLOU is actively evaluating this approach.

What are CLOU's advantages compared to other energy storage companies?

Songyu He: CLOU stands out in two key areas:

1. Deep Technical Expertise – With roots in smart metering and inverter (PCS) manufacturing, CLOU has a deep understanding of battery storage, power conversion, and grid integration.

2. Extensive Global Experience – CLOU has been active in the U.S. since 2018 and launched ERCOT projects in 2019, gaining strong expertise in international standards and regulatory compliance.

How has Midea supported CLOU's global expansion?

Songyu He: Midea has strengthened CLOU's global expansion by improving financial bankability, optimizing digital management, reducing costs, and leveraging its extensive global service network to accelerate market penetration.

 

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/exclusive-interview-with-clou-energy-storages-overseas-gm-songyu-he-key-strategies-for-global-expansion-302419637.html

SOURCE CLOU Electronics Co., Ltd.

data.title: Peace and Sport launches the #WhiteCard 2025 campaign to promote peace through sport
data.description:
Peace and Sport launches the #WhiteCard 2025 campaign to promote peace through sport

Peace and Sport launches the #WhiteCard 2025 campaign to promote peace through sport

PR Newswire

MONACO, April 3, 2025

MONACO, April 3, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- On the occasion of the International Day of Sport for Development and Peace, the Organization Peace and Sport launched the #WhiteCard 2025 campaign from Monaco. This global movement aims to highlight the role of sport as a vector of peace and social inclusion, with a particular focus on the 460 million children living in conflict zones.

To view the Multimedia News Release, please click: 
https://www.multivu.com/peace-and-sport/9328051-en-launches-its-whitecard-2025-campaign

A global call to action

According to UNICEF, one in six children lives in a conflict zone, facing the loss of access to education, the absence of playgrounds, and the risk of social exclusion. In response to this reality, Peace and Sport advocates for increased investment in sports, a tool recognized by the United Nations as essential for reconciliation, conflict prevention, and post-conflict reconstruction.

Peace education is at the heart of this initiative. Sport serves as a powerful educational tool, teaching essential values such as respect, teamwork, and resilience. By integrating sports for peace programs into educational systems, particularly in conflict-affected areas, children can regain access to structured learning environments that foster both academic and personal growth. Furthermore, sports play a crucial role in post-conflict reconstruction by helping reintegrate displaced youth, rebuild communities, and promote social cohesion. Peace and Sport urges policymakers and institutions to prioritize investment in sports for peace to ensure every child has the opportunity to learn, develop, and contribute to a peaceful and resilient society.

Support from renowned champions

The #WhiteCard 2025 campaign is supported by prominent sports figures, the Champions for Peace. Among them are Didier Drogba, Vice President of Peace and Sport, as well as Grace Geyoro (France, Football), Cheick Cissé (Ivory Coast, Taekwondo), Zakia Khudadadi (Para-Taekwondo) Siya Kolisi (South African, Rugby), Clarisse Agbegnenou (France, Judo), and Egan Bernal (Colombia, Cycling). All are committed to raising public awareness of the impact of sport in building more peaceful societies.

Join the movement

The official launch took place in Monaco in the presence of H.S.H. Prince Albert II of Monaco, a strong advocate for sport as a tool for peace and Patron of Peace and Sport.

Peace and Sport invites the international community around the world to join the movement by sharing their #WhiteCard and the message on social media. A simple gesture, yet one that carries a powerful message: sport is a right and a vital tool for building a better future.

Contact:

cb@peace-sport.org

Video - https://mma.prnewswire.com/media/2655111/Peace_and_sport.mp4
Photo - https://mma.prnewswire.com/media/2655029/Peace_and_Sport.jpg
Logo - https://mma.prnewswire.com/media/2655124/Peace_and_Sport_Logo.jpg

data.title: Tariffs Raise Inflation Uncertainty, Says ECB's De Guindos — Update
data.description:
By Paul Hannon 




  The eurozone's inflation rate is heading down toward the European Central Bank's target, but higher U.S. tariffs are a new source of uncertainty, Vice President Luis de Guindos said Thursday. 



  In a speech in Amsterdam, de Guindos said most recent data suggests the annual rate of inflation will settle at 2% "on a sustained basis." Figures released Tuesday showed the eurozone's inflation rate fell to 2.2% in March from 2.3% in February. 



  However, President Trump's Wednesday announcement of a new round of tariff increases, including a 20% duty on imports from the European Union, makes it more difficult to predict the future pace of price rises, he said. 



  "Uncertainty surrounding the inflation outlook remains high, mainly on account of increasing friction in global trade," he said. 



  De Guindos said a weakening of the euro in response to higher tariffs could push prices of imports higher, although other consequences of the increase in duties would have the opposite effect. 



  "Lower demand for euro-area exports and lower growth resulting from the impact of higher tariffs or geopolitical tensions could pose a threat to the economy, depress demand and push inflation down," he said. 



  The ECB last month lowered its key interest rate for the fifth straight meeting, and investors expect it to cut again later this month. 



  However, de Guindos said rate setters would have to be "extremely prudent" when deciding their next move. 



  "While most indicators point to inflation moving in the right direction, the environment of exceptional uncertainty requires us to stick even more closely to our data-dependent and meeting-by-meeting approach," he said. 



  De Guindos said the eurozone economy is being held back by the uncertainty created by the rewiring of the global economy instigated by the U.S. administration. 



  "An extraordinarily high level of uncertainty around economic and trade policy has been acting as a drag on markets and the economy alike," he said. 



  In addition to adjusting to tariffs from one of its main trade partners, the EU is also preparing to face the threat of Russian military aggression without the full support of its traditional ally. 



  Led by Germany, EU governments have pledged to increase military spending. But many will have to add to already high debt loads in order to do so. 



  "Despite the likely increase in debt servicing costs, public finances need to be managed in a growth-friendly way and ultimately be sustainable," de Guindos said. 





  Write to Paul Hannon at paul.hannon@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 03, 2025 04:12 ET (08:12 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
